texts.orig: Heike Mushardt 1 , Verena Kramer 2 , Deniz Hülagü 2 , Torsten Brinkmann 1, *, and Matthias Kraume 2
texts.text: Heike Mushardt 1 , Verena Kramer 2 , Deniz Hülagü 2 , Torsten Brinkmann 1, *, and Matthias Kraume 2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A new kind of gas permeation membrane is developed to separate higher hydrocarbons (C3+) from permanent gases. State of the art for such gas permeation applications are membranes based on solubility selective, rubbery polymers, which offer high permeances but relatively low selectivities. The new membrane is designed to improve selectivity, and therefore, significantly reduce energy demand and size of a gas separation plant. The membrane is based on the mixed matrix concept and consists of a rubbery polymer matrix with incorporated activated carbon particles. This two phase separation layer forms a solubility selective mixed matrix membrane by combining the advantages of both materials. The separation of nC4H10/CH4 mixtures is investigated. Based on experimental results a new concept for a transport model for mixed matrix membrane is introduced.
texts.text: A new kind of gas permeation membrane is developed to separate higher hydrocarbons (C3+) from permanent gases. State of the art for such gas permeation applications are membranes based on solubility selective, rubbery polymers, which offer high permeances but relatively low selectivities. The new membrane is designed to improve selectivity, and therefore, significantly reduce energy demand and size of a gas separation plant. The membrane is based on the mixed matrix concept and consists of a rubbery polymer matrix with incorporated activated carbon particles. This two phase separation layer forms a solubility selective mixed matrix membrane by combining the advantages of both materials. The separation of nC4H10/CH4 mixtures is investigated. Based on experimental results a new concept for a transport model for mixed matrix membrane is introduced.

texts.orig: The separation of gas streams is an important task in almost every branch of industry. The application of membrane technology has grown rapidly since the first industrial plants in the early 1980's due to its low energy demand, small plant sizes, environmental friendliness and easy operation [1]. In some processes gas permeation is already well established, including nitrogen production from air, separation of hydrogen, CO2 removal from natural gas, recovery of monomers and solvents in polymer production or separation of volatile organics from off-gases [2 - 4]. Nevertheless, the technique is not cost-competitive for all possible applications as both sufficient transmembrane fluxes and a good selectivity are necessary to realize an adequate energy demand and plant size [1]. Furthermore, the membrane material has to withstand harsh conditions regarding pressure, temperature and chemicals [5]. texts.orig: The key issue for a successful membrane process is the membrane material itself as the interaction of gas mixture components with the material determines their rate of transport [9]. Almost all commercial gas separations are currently based on dense polymeric membranes [2]. They are easily produced as thin film composite membranes on porous support structures or as hollow fibers offering good separation behavior as well as mechanical strength. Porous inorganic membranes made of ceramic, zeolite or carbon can provide even better separation properties, especially when molecular sieving can be achieved. Besides, they can be applied in high temperature operations and under harsh chemical conditions [10]. Unfortunately, inorganic membranes are difficult to produce and often very brittle. The resulting high costs per m 2 and low mechanical strength avert the industrial application of these membranes [2].

Several challenges in MMM preparation have to be dealt with concerning the interface morphology (e.g., chain rigidification, pore blockage, low adhesion) and the dispersion of particles within the polymeric matrix (e.g., agglomeration or sedimentation of particles) to form a defect-free separation layer [12]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Activated carbon (AC) has been known for decades as one of the cheapest and very effective porous sorbents to be used in adsorption processes [18]. MMM with activated carbon incorporated in polyvinyl chloride (PVC) was investigated by García et al. for the separation of H2/CH4 [19]. They prepared MMM with carbon loadings up to 60wt %, which showed an increased gas permeability but only low selectivity. Kusworo et al. studied the effect of activated carbon in polyethersulfone (PES) for the separation of oxygen from nitrogen and found an increase in selectivity for up to 5 wt % activated carbon, followed by a sharp selectivity decrease due to formation of defects caused by particle agglomeration at higher carbon loadings [20]. As far as we know no solubility selective MMM has been developed successfully by now. In this work a new MMM combining rubbery poly(octylmethylsiloxane) and activated carbon is developed for the separation of higher hydrocarbons (C3+) from permanent gases like CH4 based on solubility selective transport.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Poly(octylmethylsiloxane) (POMS) is selected as continuous polymer phase. POMS is a rubbery polymer with a glass transition temperature of -92 °C and a solubility selective character. Chemically POMS is a derivative of the wellknown poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS) with one of the methyl groups replaced by a octyl group [8]. A precursor solution with 30 wt % polymer was prepared with an appropriate cross-linking system and iso -octane (Merck KGaA, purity >99.5 %) as solvent. The cross-linking reaction was initialized via a platinum-based catalyst after adding the inorganic filler particles.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A crucial point in mixed matrix preparation is a suitable pre-treatment of filler particles as a blocking of pores by polymer chains has to be avoided. Therefore, carbon particles have been saturated with a solvent before being in contact with polymer solution. In preliminary works by Hülagü, ethanol was identified as suitable solvent for pre-saturation [22]. The ethanol used in this work was purchased from Merck KGaA (purity > 99.9 %). Different amounts of saturated carbon particles (10, 20, 40 wt %) were added to a 30wt% polymer precursor solution without catalyst and mechanically stirred (12 000 rpm, 10 min). After adding the appropriate amount of catalyst the solution was stirred again (12 000 rpm, 5 min) and degassed in an ultrasonic bath (Elmasonic S30H) for 2 min.

texts.orig: Thin film composite membranes have been prepared via solution casting on a silicone coated porous support structure consisting of non-woven polyester (PE) and porous polyacrylonitrile (PAN). Membranes were dried at 100 °C in an oven directly after casting. A thin silicone coating (0.1 wt % in iso -octane) was applied as top layer to fill eventually formed surface defects.

texts.orig: n -C4H10 (Linde AG, purity 99.95 %) in CH4 (Linde AG, purity 99.95 %). The experimental setup is depicted in Fig. 1 [23]. Samples were evacuated in a vacuum oven at 80 °C over night and then inserted to the test cell (M1). After evacuating the system, the gas mixture was filled into a feed vessel (B1). A compressor (C1) was used to achieve the desired feed pressure (10 - 40 bar) while a gas circulator (C2) generates a sufficient flow to avoid concentration polarization. The temperature of the feed mixture was controlled with a water bath (HX1) before entering the membrane test cell (M1). Experiments were performed at 20, 30 and 35°C. After equilibrating the system at the desired pressure, the pressure and flow rates of feed, permeate and retentate were measured and the gas composition on feed, permeate and retentate sides were analyzed with a gas chromatograph (Varian 3400, column Chromosorb 107). Afterwards, gas mixture was added until the next pressure step was reached.

texts.orig: The ideal adsorbed solution theory (IAST) can be used to predict binary adsorption equilibrium based on pure gas data as described in [25, 26]. It has been used to predict the binary adsorption equilibrium of n -C4H10/CH4 mixtures with different n -C4H10 concentrations (5 and 10 %). Fig. 2 shows the total adsorbed amount for the case of two different mixtures in addition to the adsorption isotherms of the pure gases. It can be seen that the binary equilibria are between the ones of the pure components, but closer to pure n -C4H10. In fact, the adsorbed phase consists mostly of n -C4H10 as illustrated in Fig. 2. For higher pressures and higher percentages of CH4 in the gas phase, the mole fraction of n -C4H10 in the adsorbed phase x n-C4H10 decreases.
texts.text: The ideal adsorbed solution theory (IAST) can be used to predict binary adsorption equilibrium based on pure gas data as described in [25, 26]. It has been used to predict the binary adsorption equilibrium of n -C4H10/CH4 mixtures with different n -C4H10 concentrations (5 and 10 %). Fig. 2 shows the total adsorbed amount for the case of two different mixtures in addition to the adsorption isotherms of the pure gases. It can be seen that the binary equilibria are between the ones of the pure components, but closer to pure n -C4H10. In fact, the adsorbed phase consists mostly of n -C4H10 as illustrated in Fig. 2. For higher pressures and higher percentages of CH4 in the gas phase, the mole fraction of n -C4H10 in the adsorbed phase x n-C4H10 decreases.

texts.orig: The performance of MMM is determined by an interplay of several parameters as for example the type and shape of filler particles, the filler content, the properties of the polymeric matrix as well as process conditions [14, 17]. Mixed gas permeation experiments with MMM were performed in this work with regard to identifying the influence of filler content (0, 10, 20, 40wt%) and particle size ( d 50 =3.5 or 1.5 l m) on permeance and selectivity. Experiments were carried out at different temperatures (20, 30, 35 °C) and feed pressures (10 - 40 bar).

texts.orig: The permeance of n -C4H10 increases for all membranes with increasing feed pressure. For condensable gases like n -C4H10 the solubility in the polymer as well as the adsorption in carbon particles increases with pressure which thus enhances the permeance [24, 28]. The dissolution of n -C4H10 in the rubbery matrix with increasing pressure induces a loosening of polymer chains [8]. This so-called swelling behavior leads to an undesired increase in CH4 permeance as well and will reduce the overall selectivity, which can be observed in Fig. 5 for pure POMS. In addition, high feed pressures can cause a compression of the separation layer which will decrease the selectivity as well.

texts.orig: An increasing amount of filler particles is reported to reduce the permeance [11]. This is confirmed by experimental results obtained in this work showing a decrease in permeance for n -C4H10 and CH4, respectively, with increasing carbon content. This reduction of permeance with higher filler contents (Fig. 4a) corresponds to an increase in membrane thickness. Besides, particles partly act as barriers as gas molecules are adsorbed and diffuse through the pores of carbon. As can be seen in Fig. 4a, the slope of n -C4H10 permeance is also reduced with higher amounts of carbon. This indicates a reduced tendency to swelling as n -C4H10 is preferentially adsorbed in carbon particles that will partly trap the gas molecules by adsorption. Thus, less n -C4H10 molecules are available to mix with the polymeric chains and cause swelling.

texts.orig: To study the effect of particle size, MMM with 20 wt % activated carbon have been produced using different grinding fractions with d 50 =3.5 l m (AC1) and d 50 =1.5 l m (AC2). As reported by Tantekin-Ersolmaz for PDMS filled with zeolites, the permeability of MMM decreases with decreasing particle size [29]. This is attributed to the increased number of particles causing more interfacial area to affect the molecular transport. In contrast, the permeance for MMM with smaller carbon particles obtained in this work is slightly higher (see Fig. 4b). It can be assumed that due to the agglomeration of particles less interfacial area is available. Besides, agglomeration causes the formation of small nonselective pinholes that enhance the permeance [17].

texts.orig: The selectivity of pure polymer membranes first increases with increasing feed pressure. At higher pressures the effects of compression and swelling are more pronounced and the selectivity levels out. The swelling is caused by dissolution of n -C4H10 in the polymeric matrix. Transport through swollen polymers follows competitive diffusion in favor of smaller molecules [30], thus, increasing the permeance of CH4.

texts.orig: The influence of carbon content on mixed gas selectivity can be seen in Fig. 5a. While MMM with 10 and 40 wt % activated car- bon show a linear increase of selectivity with increasing feed pressure, MMM with 20wt% follow the trend of pure POMS but at a higher level. At a certain pressure or rather a certain amount of n -C4H10 adsorbed in pores the n -C4H10 is assumed to form a liquid layer and by this blocks the pores for the diffusion of CH4. The results clearly show an optimum in carbon loading at 20 wt %. Membranes with lower carbon contents are probably underneath the so-called percolation threshold and particles rather act as traps than as enhancers. As the carbon loading increases, the distance between particles is reduced. By this, channels, agglomerates or even non-selective voids can be formed reducing the overall selectivity.

texts.orig: All diffusion terms are formulated according to Fick's first law. Diffusion coefficients in the polymer phase are concentration dependently calculated from permeability measurements and sorption isotherms ( n -C4H10/ D p ∼ 10 -10 m 2 s -1 , CH4/ D p ∼ 10 -9 m 2 s -1 ). Pore diffusion is considered as Knudsen diffusion since the ratio between pore size and mean free path is much smaller than one. Considering the pore filling due to adsorption, a concentration dependency for pore diffusion is taken into account ( n -C4H10/ D k ∼ 10 -8 m 2 s -1 , CH4/ D k ∼ 5 · 10 -8 m 2 s -1 ). In a first approach surface diffusion coefficients are assumed to be constant. The order of magnitude was estimated according to the model of Okazaki described by Do [25] ( n -C4H10/ D s = 4 · 10 -8 m 2 s -1 , CH4/ D s = 4·10 -7 m 2 s -1 ).

texts.text: 5 Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mixed matrix membranes have been successfully prepared with POMS as polymeric matrix and activated carbon as inorganic filler particles. Good dispersion of particles and contact to POMS matrix was observed by SEM images. Increasing the carbon content causes higher thicknesses and inhomogeneity of separation layer. The effect of carbon content and particle size on n -C4H10 permeance and n -C4H10/ CH4 mixed gas selectivity was studied. The experimental results show a decrease in permeance with increasing carbon content. The incorporation of smaller particles slightly diminishes this effect. Regarding selectivity, an optimum in carbon content at 20 wt % was found. A significant effect of particle size on selectivity was not observed. The increased selectivity of MMM compared to pure POMS offers the pos- ary conditions result from the solubility of n -C4H10 and CH4 in polymer, total pressures on feed and permeate side and condition of continuity. So far it is assumed, that n -C4H10 and CH4 do not influence the solubility of one another in the polymer.

The effect of carbon content and particle size on n -C4H10 permeance and n -C4H10/ CH4 mixed gas selectivity was studied. The experimental results show a decrease in permeance with increasing carbon content. The incorporation of smaller particles slightly diminishes this effect. Regarding selectivity, an optimum in carbon content at 20 wt % was found. A significant effect of particle size on selectivity was not observed. The increased selectivity of MMM compared to pure POMS offers the pos- ary conditions result from the solubility of n -C4H10 and CH4 in polymer, total pressures on feed and permeate side and condition of continuity. So far it is assumed, that n -C4H10 and CH4 do not influence the solubility of one another in the polymer.
